It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 156 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the purple blocks more clearly:
- Purple parts at (13,14)-(16,16), (13,20)-(16,22), (13,26)-(16,28) - 3 purple blocks
- White parts at (17,15), (17,21), (17,27) - white bottoms of purple blocks
- Yellow parts at (17,14), (17,16), (17,20), (17,22), (17,26), (17,28) - yellow eyes/dots on purple blocks
- Yellow at (38,22) - this is the player's eye (player is at row 37-38, col 20-22)
So the purple blocks are at rows 13-17, cols 14-16, 20-22, 26-28. They have purple tops, white bottoms, and yellow eyes.
The board state is:
- 4 green blocks at top (rows 1-5, cols 15, 21, 27, 33)
- 3 purple blocks (rows 13-17, cols 15, 21, 27)
- 6 green blocks in middle row (rows 31-35, cols 21, 27, 33, 39, 45, 51)
- 5 green blocks in bottom row (rows 37-41, cols 27, 33, 39, 45, 51)
- Player at (37, 20)
This is very different from the original board. The board has reorganized significantly.
I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me step back and think about what the goal might be.
In Level 1, the goal was to click on all green blocks and then move RIGHT. The level cleared when all green blocks were removed.
In Level 2, the goal might be similar, but the board reorganizes as you click. Maybe the goal is to click on all green blocks until there are none left, and the board keeps reorganizing.
But I've already clicked on 3 green blocks (bottom-left, top-left, bottom-second), and the board reorganized to have 15 green blocks (more than the original 14). So clicking on green blocks doesn't just remove them; it causes new ones to appear.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is not to click on all green blocks, but to click on specific green blocks in a specific order to trigger a level completion.
Actually, let me look at the history more carefully. In the previous turns, I was clicking on green blocks and the board was changing. Let me see if there's a pattern in how the board changes.
Actually, I think I need to reset my approach. Let me use ACTION7 to reset the level and start fresh. Then I'll try clicking on the green blocks in a different order.
Wait, ACTION7 might not reset the level. Let me check what ACTION7 does.
Actually, looking at the valid actions, ACTION7 is available. In previous levels, ACTION7 was used to reset or restart. Let me try using it to reset the level.
But wait, I've already spent 156 actions on this level. If I reset, I'll start from scratch, which might be even worse for efficiency.
Let me think about this more carefully. The current board has 15 green blocks. Maybe I need to click on all 15 to clear the level. But that would take 15 more actions, plus the 156 I've already used, which is way over the budget.
Actually, let me re-read the efficiency budget. It says "Level 2: you have used 156 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target." This means the human solution takes about 48 actions, and I've used 156. So I'm already 3.2x over, but I need to continue to clear the level.
Let me try a different approach: instead of clicking on green blocks one by one, let me see if there's a way to clear the level more efficiently. Maybe I need to move the player to a specific position, or click on the purple blocks, or do something else.
Actually, let me look at the Level 1 solution again. In Level 1, I clicked on all 7 green blocks and then moved RIGHT. The level cleared. Maybe in Level 2, I need to click on all green blocks (whatever their positions are) and then move RIGHT.
But the problem is that the board reorganizes after each click, so the positions change. I can't pre-compute all the positions.
Let me try a different approach: use ACTION7 to reset the level, then try clicking on all green blocks in a single batch (all 14 at once). If the order doesn't matter and the board doesn't reorganize until all are clicked, this might work.
Actually, I think the issue is that I don't understand the game mechanics well enough. Let me try to reset the level and start fresh, then carefully observe what happens when I click on green blocks.

<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try to reset the level using ACTION7 and start fresh
action(['ACTION7'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
